title: 2025/12/01 - TI (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-12-01 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - 2024/25 Annual Review of Crown Infrastructure Delivery [Part 2]
topic: 2024/25 Annual Review of Crown Infrastructure Delivery
description: Monday 1 December 2025 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee 2024/25 Annual Review of Crown Infrastructure Delivery Hearing of evidence: Crown Infrastructure Delivery John O'Hagan, Chief Executive Richard O'Reilly, GM Policy, Communications & Government Engagement
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual Review of Crown Infrastructure Delivery. Crown Infrastructure Delivery. John O'Hagan, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Thank you for meeting us today. Um, I just put an apology in for our board chair. Um, unfortunately she couldn't travel and couldn't be with us here today. Um, so this year, crown Infrastructure Delivery CID, has made significant progress, um, in transitioning from, um, the Christchurch focus organization of delivering the Nanka projects, following their Christchurch Earthquakes to a national, uh, agency supporting crown entities with their planning delivery, and managing their critical infrastructure across New Zealand.

Um, having completed, um, close to $1.1 billion of projects, mainly in Christchurch. Uh, CID now manages a diverse public sector portfolio nationwide, uh, addressing challenges like fragmented capability, limited capacity, uh, duplicated effort, and limited transparency.

Uh, we are recognized as a trusted, uh, agency, excelling in all aspects of infrastructure delivery. We support agencies through the entire infrastructure lifecycle. It includes planning and business case development, uh, right through to asset management and operational oversight, as well as our, um, day-to-day bread and butter delivery and management of infrastructure projects.

We are actively exploring broader opportunities, uh, to support agencies where our expertise can strengthen, uh, delivery capability and help lift performance across the infrastructure system in the country. We see real, uh, real system-wide value and concentrated infrastructure expertise within an agency like CID.

Uh, what what we see is that, um, project Governors are supported with better data for, um, for decision making, and we have, um, consistent proce, um, consistent processes and, and we provide stronger oversight in those projects that we deliver. By June, 2025, we had $1.25 billion worth of projects underway.

Uh, we supported 12 clients across client agencies across 18 sites nationwide. Our projects include the complex, uh, seismic upgrade and end of life service replacement at the Auckland District Court. That building needs to remain operational while we undertake our work there. Uh, we're also supporting the building of the science center at Kenna per for public health and forensic science, and we're doing the design and construction of the new biosecurity facility for NPI, biosecurity New Zealand and Auckland.

In the first five months of this financial year, we signed seven new cost recovery fee for service, uh, agreements with new clients. Uh, those new clients include Health New Zealand for their rapid inpatient capacity extension program, ministry for social of social development for business case and governance support for their program, developing facilities to cater for intellectually disabled people who commit serious crime and the New Zealand police as they developed their property program, uh, business case.

Um, we're also really proud to have handed over Pat Kiri, the recreation sports facility in Christchurch. It's the last, the last of anchor projects in Christchurch, and that's, uh, uh, we handed that to Christchurch City Council at the end of October. Uh, CID is now engaging with councils and local government organizations to offer our support and services, uh, to help them manage their infrastructure needs effectively.

And we've recently began work with a little port company for feasibility on a new office building, and that's on top of the work that we're doing with the Chatham Island Council, with their, um, emergency services or, and co-location of government services hub on the island. Our mission is to build and maintain adaptable, sustainable infrastructure for New Zealand.

We have high level of expertise and experience in this area, and we have excellent systems and processes. Our plan going forward is to work in partnership with our client agencies, um, both in central and local government, and to make a positive and lasting difference to New Zealand infrastructure system as the introduction.

Thanks for having us, and, um, welcome with questions. Thank you Very much. Right. Throwing up questions. Just gonna gonna start me off, Kerry. Thank you. Thank you very much, uh, for that and for coming in. Um, I'm keen to pick up on a, um, statement that you've made around your performance being linked to availability of crown funded projects, and also noting that there are only some agencies that work with you and flow through to that point later.

I'm keen to understand, first of all, if you've offered any advice around, uh, the impact of either cutting or delaying, uh, crown funded projects has had on the rollout and efficiency and the performance that you're able to provide. So we haven't had any, we don't provide any input into what projects are cut or taken forward.

Um, if that's the, I'm kind, obviously you provide a specialist service. Yeah. And I'm keen to understand, given that expertise, whether you've provided advice around impact or concern around workforce retention, things that the infrastructure sector more broadly has raised publicly. Yeah, so we've raised through conversations and open forums with infrastructure commission, um, on pipeline of projects in, uh, pipeline of projects and the, the certainty that gives, um, sector of what's coming forward.

But again, we can't influence those projects that come to market. No, no, that's fine. It's not, but we, we, we, we provide kind of advice, but it's, I think sector knows infrastructure commission knows, ministers know, uh, the impact of infrastructure on the sector. Have you noticed any impact on your projects that you are currently working on around retaining the workforce?

Um, no. Look, I I, we haven't noticed a, a decline. Um, we've got a lot of interest in the, the, uh, the projects that we go up to tender for. Mm-hmm. Um, so the sector is hungry, um, that's both in the professional services and in the, uh, construction market. So there's, there's definitely capacity in the market to do more. Okay. Uh, and we're seeing that through our tenders.

Can I, do you mind if I carry on with that? Uh, I'm keen to jump to the point I made earlier. Um, it's clear that you believe that involving yourselves with agencies to assist with their project delivery provides efficiency. Uh, therefore, can we extend that to think that it's your view that other agencies, if they were to join with you, then we would see further efficiencies in the rollout infrastructure?

I think we're seeing it with other agencies that do, um, uh, work with us. So if I can just, there's, there's two points I would like to make. There are, there are mandated agencies that need to mm-hmm. That must use us. So if they have an infrastructure need and there's some rules and requirements around what they can and can't do, they have to use us. There are other agencies that aren't mandated to use us, but if they have a capacity, uh, restraint or restriction on, on delivering their infrastructure, they can partner with us.

Health New Zealand is an example. Ministry of Education is another one where there was a, a capacity issue that we were able to fill a gap with them. So they're seeing efficiencies in what they do. Is that with the education, is that specifically the, the increased role in Auckland in The, so there was two projects that was, yeah, that was the increased, uh, short-term role growth program in Auckland. We supported them with that. And we're also working with the education on the, uh, Wellington Girls College seismic great

program of their, their Role. Okay. And, and, but you're seeing it more on a project by project basis as across to across the Yeah, that'd be fair. Do you expect that to then become the norm and perhaps those two agencies that you've worked with there? Um, look as, as they're getting, uh, possibly more funding through budget bids and their, their need to deliver increases, I see there being an a, a bigger opportunity for them to partner with us to get more efficiencies out of their, um, their delivery.

What could the risk just generally speaking, be if, if, if they chose not to? Not them specifically, just speaking generally. Oh, Look, I think we, uh, I think slower infrastructure delivery would be the obvious one. I think we can help with increased capacity, uh, within those agencies or across government sector to deliver infrastructure. Okay. Thank you. It's Useful. Can I just pick up on some of those?

So you've got, you've got a dozen, um, government agencies who are basically told they will, they will work with you. Um, raised health there, which is one of those agencies? No, no. Health, health New Zealand is not a mandated agency. Oh, Okay. It's got, sorry, ministry of Health, ministry of, okay. So not health, sorry. Got Yep.

Um, education's not on that list either. So those, so they're choosing to come, they're choosing to come to you. Okay. Yeah. So through through William Partnership, you know, we, we reach out and we offer our services, um, and we work together to arrange the working arrangements for that. So, so when, how are you measuring the satisfaction of those agencies or of any of those agencies or, and you mentioned local government as well.

How are you measuring the satisfaction of those agencies with the services you provide? And obviously one bit of satisfaction is as they come back to you again and say, Hey, well, we'll, we like that. We'll do it again. Well, look, I think return, you know, return business is definitely a, uh, a measure of satisfaction, but we've also, we reach out to the SROs within those, um, with pro within those agencies and the project they're responsible for, and we get direct feedback from those SROs.

And, and in this coming year, we're developing a client, we're developing a client satisfaction survey for, for next year to, well, that's What I was, that what I was asking Formally sort of measure that. Um, so, So that, that's the first time that this has happened. Its client satisfaction survey. Yep. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. Uh, in, in the next, uh, next time I'll take a couple

of questions before I go stick myself back on again, Simon. And then again. Thank you. Um, thanks so much. Look, you can see how you built your book. Uh, I'm really interested in, uh, how you use program management and program and project management approaches, uh, to drive these efficiencies and get value for money.

Do you want to describe some of the ways you do that, uh, that maybe aren't necessarily that or haven't been that common in public sector delivery? Uh, as, as much as they're in the private sector? Um, look, I'd say, um, we have a, a project support office, which is, uh, kind of a centralized unit within our company where, um, that does assurance across all our projects.

So they, the projects work with the PSO, um, for scheduled risk reporting, um, and also, uh, assurance on the projects, um, making sure that we're complying with their own internal management plans. Um, our data and the, the reports that we deliver or develop are, can be done in real time.

So if we need a real time risk assessment, a QRA process on a project schedule or, or financials, we can get that done really quickly within our own organization. So that brings, you know, I think that brings really good best practice to, um, program and project management. That means that governors are both at, um, our company level, but also at agency level and, and ministerial and treasury level.

They, uh, they can get real time reporting. They get accurate reporting every month. So what are the, some of the key risks that, uh, you see as common to public sector delivery that, that you're identifying, uh, across the projects or programs that you're working on? Um, look, I'd say an observation would be inconsistency of, of procurement and consistency of contract management.

Um, it's one thing that we do do well is the consistency of, of both of those. Um, so that those that tender and do work for us, they know what they're getting in both, you know, how we tender and how we, how we monitor performance on contracts. Okay. Thanks Sean. Juliet, Um, thank you so much.

I was actually wondering, like, going forward when public agencies are working with you, are they, are you like directly charging them for your assistance or, um, is some of it covered by more general crown appropriation? Um, So, um, so we've got a few clients and a few projects that working on at the moment that are covered by an appropriation.

So they're, um, historic ones, but, sorry, can you up just a little bit please? Sorry. Historic projects have been covered by an appropriation and so like the likes of ministry of Primary Industries, that's covered by an appropriation. Uh, but all new projects that we've signed up from this year onwards are all cost recovery. So we're charging agencies directly for, for that. Okay. Do you, um, how are you working or not with N fco?

Um, it seems like there's a few different agencies offering advice to agencies about infrastructure. Just wondering if like, So, so we, we work closely with them and talk to them a lot and communicate, but we have slightly different sort of areas of focus. So they're much more on the funding and financing side of things. Yeah. And we're much more on the delivery side of things, but we, we, we communicate, we make sure that when we're talking to clients, if they're actually after a more of a, a NCO thing, we'll, we'll direct them that way.

And, and likewise they do the same. So sort of a, you know, mutually um, beneficial and respectful relationship. Do you think that, um, like directly charging agencies is gonna have any impact on demand to work with you? Or, I mean, what are the, I guess, benefits of having a more direct pricing as opposed to broader?

'cause it's all public money, right? Yeah. Whether it's a broad appropriation or whether it's through Yeah, I, I guess the real benefit is that, that it, you know, clearly articulates the cost of a project. So when, when the cost of a project and doing the functions that we do, I, I covered within an agency's sort of over overall appropriation that time and effort worked on the project as opposed to other overheads and other costs.

So you more explicitly see it costs this much money to do the delivery. Um, but yeah, ultimately it is, it's public money that's coming from, you know, how quite, how it gets to us to pay the bills is, it's a sort of public policy question rather than a question. For us, There is a, an element of risk of agencies building their own capability for one-off projects.

Um, you know, they can, they can get people in quite often it's contractors at, at much higher costs, but also at the tail end of a project, when the next project, um, somewhere else is on the, um, is on the cards for those key people, they, they leave. And so the, the agency loses that, um, that IP and knowledge of the project and the closeout of a project is often quite complex.

Um, where usually us, we have that, you know, consistency of knowledge across the project and, uh, personnel within the organization that can see the project from very beginning to the very end and hand over to the end agency. Just to be clear before I go to Dan, so what you're saying is in a lot of cases, the agency itself does not have within its core capabilities, um, the skills to be able to deliver a project.

So they're contracting any way, it's just that in this, they're now saying, well, we contract to you CID as opposed to contacting to private sector. They, they, They have to build their own internal capability. Yeah. So the internal owners internal capability can be direct employees or contractor specialist services particularly, or like individuals, particularly for large complex projects, they would, you know, they could quite often Struggle. And that's, that you're saying is vulnerability.

Yeah. Yeah. Dan, Just, um, we've got this national, national infrastructure pipeline. Um, just wanting to understand, you know, the interplay between your organization and that in terms of forward planning, um, do, does your organization have cutoffs on what value projects they'll consider?

So you have mandated organizations that you work with, others that you work with contractually. Do you consider the size of the project when considering whether to engage or, Um, so, so we're open to working with, with all agencies on all projects. So we don't have a, you know, we prefer and we've got a track record in, in larger projects, um, sort of over that a hundred million sort of mark.

So a hundred million plus, but, But, but we're open and we're currently working on projects below that level as well. Um, so, um, ideally when they're a bit smaller than that, we try and package them up, um, together. Um, so like the short term real growth for education, if you put a few of those projects together, the value goes up and you can drive some efficiencies. Um, you know, it's the projects sort of management costs of a, a one, one-off sort of two or $3 million project, you know, are quite high, but you build that into a larger value than the,

the overheads and the costs sort of spread across more. So it makes more sense to both. Thank you. Just going back to the previous point around skills, um, so I'm guessing you we're really practically talking about project management, um, skills, engineering skills in-house. If you don't have them, that's when you'll come to, um, Yeah. Just to be clear, like the engineering skills aren't in the physical design of the, the building itself.

We would still, um, engage third party designers Okay. For example. But we do have engineers in-house because typically engineers make good project managers and program managers and, um, and so they oversee, um, you know, the makeup of our, our teams, uh, that, you know, those very people, project managers, right.

We've got an inhouse legal team as well, which do, um, our, uh, specialists and construction law, contract law. And so we draft our own contracts and, um, we can, um, yeah, you know, deal with those things without having to go externally and pay some big money for external consultants for that. Great. Um, do you see that there's been an opportunity to, to work in that space? And if so, where does that scope exist? What some of the barriers might be? Yeah, look, there is an opportunity and, and you know, Richard could jump in here as well. Um, uh, we, our, uh, we had to get, um, ministerial, um, approval to go and talk to local governments, which we, uh, sought at the beginning of the year.

And the minister approved us engaging with local government. We saw an opportunity to increase the, the, the services that we could offer in local government. Um, and, um, since we've started engaging with, like I mentioned at my intro, uh, we are working with the Chatham Islands Council with, uh, uh, development at they're doing down there and Little Report Company, which is a council and organization in Christchurch.

Um, we are currently working out what our offering to local gov, local government and local councils looks like, and that's a work stream that we're going on at the moment. We have reached out to, um, mayors and ES of, um, of councils offering our services as an introductory, and we'll be following that up, um, probably in the new year.

What we wanted to do is let the local body election settle, get the, um, councils established, and then we'd pick that up in the new year. So that's the main. In terms of your engagement in this space to date, have you identified any particular funding constraints that exist within the sector that might have an issue with your ability to be able to sort of, um, lean into that, that scope opportunity?

The, the funding like in central government is, is, you know, hard to come by in local government as well, so there's always constraints. Um, and, but again, we don't sort of, you know, we, we can't fix that problem, so we're just there to make sure that, um, when there's projects that need delivery, we can help them deliver them most efficiently and effectively as they can. Um, the, the funding questions are outside our scope.

So the, the capital intensive agencies, as we've talked about, health New Zealand Ministry of Education, um, and a couple of others aren't required to use us, but increasingly they are seeing the value from using us.

Um, so there is a risk that if they don't choose to, then they will keep doing the way things have have been done in the past. Um, but you know, that's, that's getting increasingly better over time, their performance in those spaces. So If you have more projects or demand for your services than you have capacity, how do you prioritize which ones you work on?

Oh, we, we would grow, um, if we can get involved in a project early enough, like business case, there's enough time for us to then see the, it's a different team that does the business case that does the actual delivery of the project. NZTA, um, and not a mandated agency and they've got a delivery capability? New water entities, Yeah, we see that as an opportunity. That's useful. Thank you. I've got, um, Kieran, Simon, David, thank you. There's, you know, you, you always have to manage contingencies and project budgets, but none of them are over budget. It's, it's so in time. Yeah, sure. Um, uh, I don't think anything's off the table, but, um, we, we have to just pick our paths, uh, as we grow, you know, we're in year two now.

Mm-hmm. Uh, we have to be careful. We, No one's gonna quote you as saying we do it better than NZTA, that's alright, but we're in, we're in private here for the committee to understand of what you provide. Uh, and then it's for others to make those sorts of, Yeah, again, the, the mandate would help.

Um, you know, the mandate has, has some boundaries and, and, and, you know, we work with those agencies within those boundaries. Um, working with agencies outside of that, uh, of the mandate, you know, it takes a one in partnership to, to get those over lined. And yeah. And as time is going on, the likes of education and health are seeing the benefits now, uh, and, uh, are starting to engage more.

Uh, you mentioned water earlier, would you include local transport networks? Uh, uh, that's one of the, one of the local government things that we're looking at, yes. Okay. Thank you. Thank you. Mr. Is yours, is yours a sub off this? Um, well, it's basic FaceTime. They're all on the same space. They're all like just a rolling sub.

Um, new creation. You mentioned your involvement in business casing and we often hear from ministers that it concerned how long it takes for business cases to go from problem to solution and whether the business case actually reflects the best options and not.

So what insights could you share with this committee about, uh, improvements that could be made to business case approach? 'cause we, we've seen single stage business cases, which actually sum up a problem and recommended procurement solution and move to delivery quite quickly, but it needs must, uh, and other business cases that drag on, um, for years and years and often produce some some pretty underwhelming recommendations.

So what, what might you be able to share with us? I mean, there's a range of things there. The, the system is quite complicated at the moment. Um, and sometimes business cases are developed with a, you know, an end outcome in mind as opposed to clearly identifying what the problem is and what the range of solutions are. Right? Um, so if you're sort of trying to backfill a business case to suit a predetermined outcome, that can be problematic.

Um, but doing the work early in the business case and process, working through the options, spending time on, on getting those costings and things well worked through early on, mm-hmm. Um, does save you time and money in the long run, but there is a temptation to sort of rush it through the process, get a business case quickly to meet a, a budget timeframe or, or whatever.

Um, and then realize that actually the work hasn't been done. And so it then blows out a cost wise or needs further work. So, um, spending that time up front in the planning is really pays dividend down the down the line. Okay, thanks David. So I suppose, uh, Simon's question was sort of the upfront importance of the business case, and mine's sort of more at the end of project and you've just, uh, obviously, uh, got to the report phase with, uh, part Q and probably the question is around, uh, capturing learnings of that project for other, you know, like, uh,

Karen mentioned NZT, how, how do they learn of actually what you're learning with these major, major projects itself? Yeah, so we, we've done lessons learned through, through the lifecycle of the projects. Um, give an example of when we finished the convention center to PI Convention Center in Christchurch. There was some lessons learned there that we wanted to pull across to Par Curie.

And so we, you know, we don't wait till the end of the project to capture those, those lessons. Uh, uh, and with parti, for example, we've already completed the procurement, um, and tender process, uh, lessons learned. So we've captured that information and data. We create reports, so we've got a series of, of those reports that we keep, um, that we've got in house and we use those lessons across the procurement activities that we do for our next project.

And the one after, are those Reports internally focused or are they there for external as well? Oh, look at external, could look at them. We haven't kind of published them externally, um, but they are, there're an internal, there are very critical internal focus of our own like, review and, and, and, um, of those projects so that we do better the next time. Okay.

Thank you. Done? Yep. Thank you Mr. Chair. Um, so just thinking about this wrap up and wash up and contract close out, uh, do you have any involvement in assessing benefits realization that the completion of a project, because sometimes we know that clients are very happy to have the ribbon cut and someone moves into the facility, and then not necessarily a lot of looking back at the original business case and whether we got what we asked for and what we've paid for.

Is it something you guys have an involvement in? Uh, look, really good question. Um, the short answer is no. When we've finished the projects either at practical completion, them handed, handed them over to the asset owner, mm, the benefits and some of these projects that we deliver are years in the making. They're not kind of like immediate, um, benefits. Uh, and so it'll be over to the end owner to capture those business case.

At the end of the day, uh, we are delivering a project for a project owner. They were, they own the business case. They own the, the benefits from the business case. Mm-hmm. We deliver, we hand the asset back that'll be best placed to then measure the benefits of that project against the business case in time. Okay. Thanks. Um, I look, I'll, I'll go to you in a sec.

Just pick up something there. So just going back to the business case issue. So we've, we've had quite a bit of critiquing, probably particularly from infrastructure commission, but from others as well about the, about the actual costing of the projects in the first place. And so that is part of what you pick, you pick up at some stage. You said that none of the budgets are under pressure. Somebody set a budget at some point in time.

Quite often we end up with projects which somebody says, okay, projects, we, we wanna build a, you know, a white elephant, um, you know, how much is it gonna cost? Somebody puts a finger in the air and says, well, um, I think it's gonna cost this. They've done no geotech, they've done no design, they've done, you know, they haven't tested the market, haven't done any of that. And then when they actually do do that, it comes back and says, well, it's gonna cost you three times, five times, 10 times what it was expected to cost.

Have you got any observations about that part of the process and setting the, you know, the budgets in the first place? Um, in some of the larger projects where, um, the project owner owns the business case, they're going through the business case process, we are pushing ourselves under the commercial and management cases so that we can challenge the, the commercials and the financials of, of the makeup of the projects.

Uh, and the ones that we've done, we have insisted on kind of early works doing investigation work so that we get an idea of what the geotech, uh, conditions are like, uh, on those projects so that we can get better costings. Um, we also push, um, uh, push hard on, on, uh, contingency and making sure that there's adequate contingencies, uh, through the, through the lifecycle of the project, uh, including escalation, particularly when some of these larger projects can, can take, uh, significant years to, to uh, go through, uh,

the design process, but also the build process and escalation is a true cost. So making sure that that's built into to those project budgets. So the ones that we've been involved in, we have, we have what we would like to say we've influenced some of the, the outcomes of what those financials were like. So, so does that mean, let's say we've decided this white, well white elephant is gonna be a hundred million dollars as the original proposition you get involved and say, well actually no, we think actually it needs

to be two or 300, let's say, well let's called 200. Um, you push back on that, what's the process that they go through as a, as a client to go, ah, okay, well we might have an issue here. Oh, the process would be that they, they would, could be peer review of the costings that would go out and get a peer review that to double check the, the original, um, values that are in the business case.

So that's a, that's a, a quite a typical process that we So you, but you would not feel comfortable taking on a project, which they, you in your view? They clearly got wrong. Uh, We would be trying to influence, you know, we, we provide our, our due diligence review. If we think that the time and costs are, um, are under pressure in a business case, we would push hard to actually get them to re-look at that and make sure that we've got the right, um, time and costs in those business cases we wouldn't want to take a project on if we, that's what I,
